question_text: Suppose that X has the probability density function $ f ( x ) = { \left\{ \begin{array} { l l } { { \frac { \lambda ^ { \alpha } } { \Gamma ( \alpha ) } } x ^ { \alpha - 1 } e ^ { - \lambda x } } & { { \mathrm { i f ~ } } x > 0 } \\ { 0 } & { { \mathrm { o t h e r w i s e , } } } \end{array} \right. } $ where $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$ . Which one of the following statements is NOT true?

options.B: Variance of X exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

solution:
1) Understanding the probability density function:

This is the probability density function of a Gamma distribution with shape parameter α and rate parameter λ. The mean and variance of a Gamma-distributed random variable are well-defined for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

2) Analysis of the options:

(A) $E ( X )$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0 \colon$

This is true because the mean of a Gamma distribution exists for $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

(B) Variance of X exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0 { : }$

This is true because the variance of a Gamma distribution exists for $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

(C) $E \left( { \frac { 1 } { X } } \right)$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0 { : }$

This is NOT true. For small values of $X , { \frac { 1 } { X } }$ becomes very large, and the expectation $E \left( { \frac { 1 } { X } } \right)$ does not exist for all values of $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

(D) $E ( \log ( 1 + X ) )$ exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0 \mathrm { : }$

This is true. The logarithmic transformation of a Gamma distribution’s expectation exists for all $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$

The correct answer is (C).
Quick Tip

\- The expectation of $\textstyle { \frac { 1 } { X } }$ does not always exist for all values of $\alpha > 0$ and $\lambda > 0$ due to the behavior of X near 0.

\- Expectation functions involving logarithms or reciprocals can have existence conditions based on the tail behavior of the distribution.
